steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The astronomer claims she can see 'most, but not all' stars using a 160 mm aperture. The correct data must show exactly one star requiring more than 160 mm, while the rest require 160 mm or less.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — Only Sk-69°249 A can be seen from Earth using a telescope with a 60 mm aperture.: This is about a 60 mm aperture, not 160 mm, and says nothing about whether most stars are visible at 160 mm. ✗
  • Choice B — Only Sk-69°249 A has a mass below 145 solar masses.: Mass is unrelated to aperture requirements, so this cannot support the astronomer's claim about what she can see. ✗
  • Choice C — Only HSH95-46 requires a telescope with a minimum aperture greater than 160 mm to be visible from Earth.: Exactly one star (HSH95-46) exceeds the 160 mm threshold, so all the others are visible — that is 'most, but not all.' ✓
  • Choice D — Only VFTS 482 is further than 163,000 light-years from Earth.: Distance from Earth is not the same as aperture requirement, so this does not address what the 160 mm telescope can see. ✗
That matches Choice C — Only HSH95-46 requires a telescope with a minimum aperture greater than 160 mm to be visible from Earth..
